questions.question: What is the clearest success metric for an alert routing system?
options: The right responder is reached quickly with minimal duplicate interruption.
The platform emits the highest possible number of notifications.
Every alert is sent to every engineer for safety.
No alert is ever grouped with another alert.
questions.explanation: Signal quality and correct human routing are the real goals.

questions.question: Why is deduplication important in paging systems?
options: It reduces repeated pages for the same underlying issue.
It removes the need for escalation policies.
It guarantees all incidents are resolved faster.
It replaces ownership metadata.
questions.explanation: Dedup protects responders from noisy duplicates.

questions.question: Which metric best exposes poor on-call signal quality?
options: Escalation rate and acknowledgement time by severity and team.
Average keyboard brand used by responders.
The number of emojis in chat channels.
Median length of runbook URLs.
questions.explanation: Escalation and ack behavior reveal whether routing is working.

questions.question: Why should ownership metadata be mandatory for production services?
options: Because sev-1 routing fails badly when no clear team owns the service.
Because it lowers CPU usage on notification providers.
Because it makes maintenance windows unnecessary.
Because it eliminates duplicate alerts.
questions.explanation: Unowned services are dangerous during incidents.

questions.question: What trade-off comes with aggressive grouping rules?
options: Fewer pages reach humans, but unrelated failures may be merged too broadly.
Guaranteed perfect root-cause detection.
No need for acknowledgement tracking.
Lower need for provider redundancy.
questions.explanation: Grouping reduces noise but can hide distinction.

questions.question: Which failure is most directly mitigated by multi-provider notification support?
options: One paging vendor or channel failing to deliver notifications.
An engineer forgetting a command-line flag.
A dashboard taking too long to load.
A metric name changing case.
questions.explanation: Provider diversity reduces notification delivery risk.

questions.question: Why is cached last-good schedule data useful?
options: It lets routing continue if the source-of-truth schedule system is slow or temporarily unavailable.
It guarantees schedules are always perfectly current.
It removes the need for incident suppression.
It prevents alert storms at the source.
questions.explanation: Routing needs resilience against schedule-system issues.

questions.question: What is the better fallback when a sev-1 alert cannot be matched to an owner?
options: Route to a defined central fallback path and record the ownership failure loudly.
Drop the alert because the policy was incomplete.
Wait for the next business day to ask around.
Notify every employee by default.
questions.explanation: Fallback routing plus auditability is safer than silent failure.
